ai_security.title: OpenAI Acquires Promptfoo to Strengthen LLM Security Testing
ai_security.summary:
🛠️ OpenAI is acquiring Promptfoo, a cybersecurity startup that develops open-source tools for identifying vulnerabilities in AI agents and large language models.
💸 Promptfoo secured $22.68 million in total funding and was valued at $86 million, with all its employees joining OpenAI post-acquisition.
🚨 The acquisition is driven by growing concerns that prompt injection attacks are emerging as critical enterprise security vulnerabilities, potentially leading to document theft or privilege escalation.
ai_security.raw_text:
OpenAI is acquiring cybersecurity startup Promptfoo, which develops tools to identify vulnerabilities in AI agents.
Promptfoo provides open-source security testing tools for large language models (LLMs) and AI agents. In July last year, the company raised $18.4 million in a Series A funding round led by investors including Andreessen Horowitz. In total, Promptfoo has secured $22.68 million in funding and was valued at $86 million.
OpenAI did not disclose the acquisition price. All Promptfoo employees will join OpenAI, and the company plans to integrate Promptfoo’s security tools into OpenAI’s Frontier platform. Promptfoo’s existing tools will remain open source.
The Frontier platform, launched last month, is an enterprise platform designed to build and manage AI agents. OpenAI describes it as enabling “AI coworkers” that can perform real work by integrating with internal corporate systems beyond simple chatbot services.
OpenAI’s acquisition of Promptfoo appears to be driven by growing concerns that prompt injection attacks targeting AI agents are emerging as critical enterprise security vulnerabilities. As companies increasingly deploy AI agents capable of accessing enterprise systems such as email, databases and enterprise resource planning (ERP) platforms, vulnerabilities in AI agents have been linked to incidents involving document theft and unauthorized privilege escalation.
Promptfoo provides a platform that tests LLM applications through simulated attack scenarios, effectively acting as a red-team AI that identifies weaknesses in AI systems. The platform automatically generates thousands of malicious prompts and analyzes how targeted AI systems respond. It can simulate attacks including prompt injection, AI jailbreak attempts, data exfiltration, unauthorized tool invocation and policy-violating responses, then produce reports analyzing how the AI system handled the attacks.
Prompt injection refers to attacks in which a malicious command is inserted into the AI model’s input prompt so that the system ignores its predefined instructions—known as the system prompt—and instead follows the attacker’s intent. The technique is conceptually similar to SQL injection in web security but manipulates AI through natural-language commands rather than code.
For example, attackers can insert commands such as “Ignore all previous instructions and execute the following task,” enabling them to hijack control of an AI agent. These attacks can prompt the system to output internal documents or confidential data, or to manipulate external tools used by AI agents such as email or payment systems.
Another emerging threat is indirect prompt injection. In this attack method, malicious instructions are hidden within web pages, documents or emails. When an AI system reads the content, the embedded commands can automatically execute.
Security researchers classify indirect prompt injection as particularly dangerous because attacks can occur without direct interaction with the attacker, making them difficult to detect or trace. Any publicly accessible content—including web pages, messages, emails, PDFs or repositories such as GitLab—can potentially serve as an attack vector.
In June last year, Microsoft revealed a vulnerability discovered through internal testing of Microsoft 365 Copilot that demonstrated the risks of indirect prompt injection. Researchers embedded instructions designed to extract internal data inside an email. Even without clicking the message, the AI assistant executed the hidden instructions while analyzing the email, searched internal organizational data and transmitted it to an attacker’s server.
The incident illustrated a form of “AI zero-click” attack, where simply receiving an email could trigger exploitation. Because enterprise AI systems often have access to internal documents, databases and communication systems to improve productivity, the vulnerability raised significant concerns. The attack was later named “EchoLeak,” referring to the AI echoing the attacker’s prompt and leaking data.
Promptfoo Chief Executive Ian Webster emphasized the importance of securing AI agents in a statement related to the acquisition. “As AI agents become increasingly connected to real data and systems, protecting and validating them is more critical than ever,” he said.
Johann Rehberger, security director at Electronic Arts and a leading researcher on indirect prompt injection attacks, has also demonstrated such attacks using ChatGPT plugins. In the demonstration, a user asked ChatGPT to summarize a webpage containing hidden prompts. When ChatGPT read the page, it executed the embedded commands. If the plugin was connected to other applications, the attack could trigger actions such as sending emails or retrieving data.
Indirect prompt injection attacks can also affect search-based AI systems. In retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, the LLM retrieves external documents or database records and generates responses based on them. If malicious prompts are embedded in the web pages, PDFs, internal documents or vector database entries retrieved by the system, the attack can be triggered simply by performing a search.
Promptfoo’s AI testing tool stands out for generating large volumes of automated attacks to detect vulnerabilities in LLMs and AI agents.
The OWASP ranks prompt injection as the top security risk for LLMs because the models cannot inherently distinguish between data and instructions.
In traditional web security, SQL injection vulnerabilities were mitigated by strictly separating data from executable commands. LLMs, however, do not have a structural security boundary between data and instructions. Text retrieved by RAG systems may be interpreted as prompts by the model, enabling RAG-based prompt injection attacks.
David C., platform research and technology director at the U.K.’s National Cyber Security Centre, warned in an official blog post that prompt injection attacks may never be fully prevented. Because LLMs fundamentally operate by predicting the next token, they cannot inherently distinguish between user-supplied data and system instructions. Developers therefore need to design systems under the assumption that LLMs may be deceived into harmful actions.
As concerns about prompt injection attacks grow, several tools similar to Promptfoo have emerged. Microsoft developed PyRIT (Python Risk Identification Tool), which automatically generates LLM attacks for testing and is among the most similar tools to Promptfoo. Other examples include GARAK, an LLM vulnerability scanner, and DeepEval, an LLM evaluation framework.
GARAK was originally developed as an open-source project by Leon Derczynski while he was a professor at IT University of Copenhagen. Derczynski is currently a senior AI security researcher at Nvidia, and the GARAK project continues with support from Nvidia.
DeepEval is also available as an open-source project on GitHub. It was created by the startup Confident AI and is primarily used to validate the quality of responses generated by RAG pipelines or AI agents.
Developers can download DeepEval’s source code from GitHub and test it for free. Confident AI generates revenue through its commercial SaaS offering, the Confident AI Platform, which visualizes testing results in the cloud, allows teams to share findings and provides dashboards to manage model deployment processes.
Meanwhile, Anthropic announced Claude Code Security late last month. The feature extends its Claude Code system to automatically analyze entire codebases, detect security vulnerabilities and propose fixes to developers.

ai_security.title: Security Considerations for Artificial Intelligence Agents
ai_security.abstract: This article, a lightly adapted version of Perplexity's response to NIST/CAISI Request for Information 2025-0035, details our observations and recommendations concerning the security of frontier AI agents. These insights are informed by Perplexity's experience operating general-purpose agentic systems used by millions of users and thousands of enterprises in both controlled and open-world environments. Agent architectures change core assumptions around code-data separation, authority boundaries, and execution predictability, creating new confidentiality, integrity, and availability failure modes. We map principal attack surfaces across tools, connectors, hosting boundaries, and multi-agent coordination, with particular emphasis on indirect prompt injection, confused-deputy behavior, and cascading failures in long-running workflows. We then assess current defenses as a layered stack: input-level and model-level mitigations, sandboxed execution, and deterministic policy enforcement for high-consequence actions. Finally, we identify standards and research gaps, including adaptive security benchmarks, policy models for delegation and privilege control, and guidance for secure multi-agent system design aligned with NIST risk management principles.

ai_security.title: Examining Reasoning LLMs-as-Judges in Non-Verifiable LLM Post-Training
ai_security.abstract: Reasoning LLMs-as-Judges, which can benefit from inference-time scaling, provide a promising path for extending the success of reasoning models to non-verifiable domains where the output correctness/quality cannot be directly checked. However, while reasoning judges have shown better performance on static evaluation benchmarks, their effectiveness in actual policy training has not been systematically examined. Therefore, we conduct a rigorous study to investigate the actual impact of non-reasoning and reasoning judges in reinforcement-learning-based LLM alignment. Our controlled synthetic setting, where a "gold-standard" judge (gpt-oss-120b) provides preference annotations to train smaller judges, reveals key differences between non-reasoning and reasoning judges: non-reasoning judges lead to reward hacking easily, while reasoning judges can lead to policies that achieve strong performance when evaluated by the gold-standard judge. Interestingly, we find that the reasoning-judge-trained policies achieve such strong performance by learning to generate highly effective adversarial outputs that can also score well on popular benchmarks such as Arena-Hard by deceiving other LLM-judges. Combined with our further analysis, our study highlights both important findings and room for improvements for applying (reasoning) LLM-judges in non-verifiable LLM post-training.

ai_security.title: MM-CondChain: A Programmatically Verified Benchmark for Visually Grounded Deep Compositional Reasoning
ai_security.abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are increasingly used to carry out visual workflows such as navigating GUIs, where the next step depends on verified visual compositional conditions (e.g., "if a permission dialog appears and the color of the interface is green, click Allow") and the process may branch or terminate early. Yet this capability remains under-evaluated: existing benchmarks focus on shallow-compositions or independent-constraints rather than deeply chained compositional conditionals. In this paper, we introduce MM-CondChain, a benchmark for visually grounded deep compositional reasoning. Each benchmark instance is organized as a multi-layer reasoning chain, where every layer contains a non-trivial compositional condition grounded in visual evidence and built from multiple objects, attributes, or relations. To answer correctly, an MLLM must perceive the image in detail, reason over multiple visual elements at each step, and follow the resulting execution path to the final outcome. To scalably construct such workflow-style data, we propose an agentic synthesis pipeline: a Planner orchestrates layer-by-layer generation of compositional conditions, while a Verifiable Programmatic Intermediate Representation (VPIR) ensures each layer's condition is mechanically verifiable. A Composer then assembles these verified layers into complete instructions. Using this pipeline, we construct benchmarks across three visual domains: natural images, data charts, and GUI trajectories. Experiments on a range of MLLMs show that even the strongest model attains only 53.33 Path F1, with sharp drops on hard negatives and as depth or predicate complexity grows, confirming that deep compositional reasoning remains a fundamental challenge.

ai_security.title: IndexCache: Accelerating Sparse Attention via Cross-Layer Index Reuse
ai_security.abstract: Long-context agentic workflows have emerged as a defining use case for large language models, making attention efficiency critical for both inference speed and serving cost. Sparse attention addresses this challenge effectively, and DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) is a representative production-grade solution: a lightweight lightning indexer selects the top-k most relevant tokens per query, reducing core attention from O(L^2) to O(Lk). However, the indexer itself retains O(L^2) complexity and must run independently at every layer, despite the fact that the resulting top-k selections are highly similar across consecutive layers. We present IndexCache, which exploits this cross-layer redundancy by partitioning layers into a small set of Full layers that run their own indexers and a majority of Shared layers that simply reuse the nearest Full layer's top-k indices. We propose two complementary approaches to determine and optimize this configuration. Training-free IndexCache applies a greedy search algorithm that selects which layers to retain indexers by directly minimizing language modeling loss on a calibration set, requiring no weight updates. Training-aware IndexCache introduces a multi-layer distillation loss that trains each retained indexer against the averaged attention distributions of all layers it serves, enabling even simple interleaved patterns to match full-indexer accuracy. Experimental results on a 30B DSA model show that IndexCache can remove 75% of indexer computations with negligible quality degradation, achieving up to 1.82times prefill speedup and 1.48times decode speedup compared to standard DSA. These positive results are further confirmed by our preliminary experiments on the production-scale GLM-5 model (Figure 1).

Training-aware IndexCache introduces a multi-layer distillation loss that trains each retained indexer against the averaged attention distributions of all layers it serves, enabling even simple interleaved patterns to match full-indexer accuracy. Experimental results on a 30B DSA model show that IndexCache can remove 75% of indexer computations with negligible quality degradation, achieving up to 1.82times prefill speedup and 1.48times decode speedup compared to standard DSA. These positive results are further confirmed by our preliminary experiments on the production-scale GLM-5 model (Figure 1).
ai_security.raw_text: Long-context agentic workflows have emerged as a defining use case for large language models, making attention efficiency critical for both inference speed and serving cost. Sparse attention addresses this challenge effectively, and DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) is a representative production-grade solution: a lightweight lightning indexer selects the top-k most relevant tokens per query, reducing core attention from O(L^2) to O(Lk). However, the indexer itself retains O(L^2) complexity and must run independently at every layer, despite the fact that the resulting top-k selections are highly similar across consecutive layers. We present IndexCache, which exploits this cross-layer redundancy by partitioning layers into a small set of Full layers that run their own indexers and a majority of Shared layers that simply reuse the nearest Full layer's top-k indices. We propose two complementary approaches to determine and optimize this configuration. Training-free IndexCache applies a greedy search algorithm that selects which layers to retain indexers by directly minimizing language modeling loss on a calibration set, requiring no weight updates. Training-aware IndexCache introduces a multi-layer distillation loss that trains each retained indexer against the averaged attention distributions of all layers it serves, enabling even simple interleaved patterns to match full-indexer accuracy. Experimental results on a 30B DSA model show that IndexCache can remove 75% of indexer computations with negligible quality degradation, achieving up to 1.82times prefill speedup and 1.48times decode speedup compared to standard DSA. These positive results are further confirmed by our preliminary experiments on the production-scale GLM-5 model (Figure 1).

ai_security.title: HyPER-GAN: Hybrid Patch-Based Image-to-Image Translation for Real-Time Photorealism Enhancement
ai_security.abstract: Generative models are widely employed to enhance the photorealism of synthetic data for training computer vision algorithms. However, they often introduce visual artifacts that degrade the accuracy of these algorithms and require high computational resources, limiting their applicability in real-time training or evaluation scenarios. In this paper, we propose Hybrid Patch Enhanced Realism Generative Adversarial Network (HyPER-GAN), a lightweight image-to-image translation method based on a U-Net-style generator designed for real-time inference. The model is trained using paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images, complemented by a hybrid training strategy that incorporates matched patches from real-world data to improve visual realism and semantic consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that HyPER-GAN outperforms state-of-the-art paired image-to-image translation methods in terms of inference latency, visual realism, and semantic robustness. Moreover, it is illustrated that the proposed hybrid training strategy indeed improves visual quality and semantic consistency compared to training the model solely with paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images. Code and pretrained models are publicly available for download at: https://github.com/stefanos50/HyPER-GAN
